data.title: Ethereum Price Could Surge Toward $6,000 as it Follows Key Technical Pattern
data.description:
The price of the second-largest cryptocurrency by market capitalization, Ethereum’s ether, could soon surge toward the $6,000 mark as it’s currently following a key technical pattern.

According to popular cryptocurrency analyst Ali Martinez, ether is following as ascending parallel channel, which could mean that a dip to the $2,800 mark would lead to a massive price rise toward the $6,000 level.

Ethereum is at the time of writing trading at $3,300 after losing around 10% of its value over the last 30 days.

Ali
@ali_charts

If #Ethereum BINANCE:ETHUSDT is following an ascending parallel channel, a dip to the lower boundary at $2,800 could act as a launchpad for a move toward $6,000. pic.twitter.com/uYP6BW3DZh

Jan 10, 2025

As CryptoGlobe reported, while the price of bitcoin has outperformed ether over the last few months, data from cryptocurrency analytics firm IntoTheBlock shows that the percentage of long-term ether holders has consistently outpaced that of bitcoin holders throughout 2024, in a noteworthy divergence given the price performance both assets saw over the last 12 months.

The data shows that the proportion of long-term BTC holders has declined through the year to now stand at 62.3%, while that of long-term ETH holders edged up to now stand above 75%.

It’s worth noting that the ether supply has declined by approximately 0.02% over the past two years since the network’s merger with the Beacon Chain, which transitioned it to a Proof-of-Stake consensus algorithm.

According to data from Ultrasound Money, a platform that monitors Ethereum’s supply changes, a total of 53,514.62 ETH has been burned since the network’s merger. This equates to approximately $175.9 million worth of the second-largest digital asset by market capitalization being burned since the upgrade over two years ago.

Currently, the ether supply stands at around 120.4 million ETH, and the network’s supply has been gradually decreasing by 0.02% annually since the upgrade. Over the past 30 days, lower usage has resulted in a positive supply growth of 0.31% for the year. Consequently, 947,000 ETH are issued annually, while annualized burns amount to 575,000 ETH, as per the platform’s data.

Featured image via Unsplash.

data.title: Rumble to Provide Cloud Services to El Salvador
data.description:
By Connor Hart

Rumble inked a cloud-services agreement with El Salvador's government, under which the video platform said it will provide the country with services including cloud storage and computing.

The company on Friday said the deal represents "the increasing migration of the world toward companies that align more with the values of freedom that Rumble represents."

Terms of the deal were not disclosed.

Vice President-Elect JD Vance invested in Rumble in 2021, alongside a group of prominent conservative venture capitalists, including Peter Thiel. The company's deal with El Salvador comes after the country made bitcoin an official currency, though much of the plan was scaled back last month in exchange for a $1.4 billion loan by the International Monetary Fund.

Chief Executive Chris Pavlovski said the deal shows Rumble's infrastructure and capabilities are suitable for governments to trust for data and computing needs.

"This is also just more proof that the world recognizes the importance of independence from Big Tech, as no one wants to risk being technologically silenced because an activist in Silicon Valley doesn't like what you think or say," he said.

Big tech companies have been embracing Donald Trump's upcoming presidential terms. Earlier this week, Meta Platforms said it is ending fact-checking and removing restrictions on speech across Facebook and Instagram, a move CEO Mark Zuckerberg described as an attempt to restore free expression on its platforms.

Write to Connor Hart at connor.hart@wsj.com

data.title: NioCorp Developments 2Q Loss/Shr 1c >NB
Biogen proposes to buy remaining stake in Sage in $442 million deal
data.description:
Drugmaker Biogen will buy all outstanding shares of Sage Therapeutics that it does not already own for $7.22 apiece, a filing showed on Friday, sending the latter's stock up 34% in extended trade.

As per the filing, Biogen has a 10.2% stake in Sage Therapeutics. The offer price, representing a 30% premium to the stock's close on Friday, values Sage's equity at $441.7 million, according to Reuters calculations.

Biogen has been navigating a slow pick-up in sales for its Alzheimer's drug Leqembi in the United States after concerns over cost, efficacy and side effects.

Sage, whose shares fell 74.9% last year, abandoned the development of its drug dalzanemdor after multiple trial failures. The company said in November that it will focus on its postpartum depression drug, Zurzuvae, which it developed in partnership with Biogen.

In July, Sage also scrapped the development of another neurological disorder drug it was developing with Biogen.

data.title: Newpath Resources Reports Voting Results from Annual General Meeting
data.description:
Vancouver, British Columbia--(Newsfile Corp. - January 10, 2025) - Newpath Resources Inc. (FSE: 0MZ) (OTC Pink: RDYFF) ("Newpath" or the "Company"), is pleased to announce the voting results from the Company's Annual General Meeting of the Shareholders (the "Meeting") held on January 10, 2025. A total of 4,193,601 common shares were represented at the Meeting in person or by proxy, representing 21.11% of all outstanding common shares. All matters submitted to shareholders for approval as set out in the Company's Notice of Meeting and Information Circular (the "Circular"), both dated November 26, 2024, were approved by the requisite majority of votes cast at the Meeting.

Election of Directors

Each of the incumbent members of the board of directors, being Alexander McAulay, Darren Collins, and Gerhard Merkel, were re-elected.

Appointment of Auditor

De Visser Gray LLP was re-appointed as the Company's auditors for the ensuing year with the remuneration to be fixed by the directors.

About Newpath Resources Inc.

Newpath is a Canadian exploration company listed on the Canadian Securities Exchange under the symbol PATH. As of December 2024, Newpath's early-stage Orefield critical metal exploration project is composed of two claim groups (Alpha/Bravo and Delta), totals approximately 469 square kilometres) west and southwest of Lake Nipigon in Northern Ontario, approximately 50 kilometres northeast of Thunder Bay. The company also owns the Northshore gold project located in the Schreiber-Hemlo greenstone belt.

data.title: Soybeans Post Rally on USDA Data Day
data.description:
Soybeans closed out the Friday session rallying 9 ¾ to 26 ½ cents across the board, led by the nearbys on bullish USDA data. March was 33 ½ cents higher on the day. CmdtyView’s national front month Cash Bean price was up 24 3/4 cents at $9.68 1/4. Soymeal futures were down 60 cents to $2.40/ton on the day, with March down $10.30 on the week. Soy Oil futures were rallying 211 to 282 points higher on the session, with March up 565 points this week. The Biden Administration released partial guidance on the 45Z tax credits today, but did not finalize any details. 

This morning’s Crop Production report showed a 1 bpa drop to yield at 50.7 bpa for soybeans, well below estimates. Harvested acres were trimmed by 250,000 acres to 86.05 million, which added to the drop in production of 95 mbu to 4.366 bbu. With the smaller supplies heading in, the December 1 stocks total was tallied at 3.099 bbu, 112 mbu below estimates and up just 98 mbu from last year. The WASDE ending stocks projection was trimmed by 90 mbu to 380 mbu, 77 mbu below estimates. Most of the demand side of the balance sheet was left unchanged, with imports up 5 mbu. 

On the world side, USDA left the Brazilian and Argentine numbers alone. Ending stocks for the world were tightened to 128.37 MMT below December by 3.5 MMT and well below estimates that were calling for an increase. 

This morning’s Export Sales report tallied 2024/25 soybean sales at just 288,671 MT, coming in at a MY low and below the 0.4 to 1.3 MMT range of estimates in the week of January 2. The Netherlands purchased a total of 205,400 MT, with 171,800 MT sold to China. A total of 696,100 MT in net reductions was reported by unknown destinations, with most switched to a destination. 

Meal sales totaled 144,918 MT in that week, the lowest for the marketing year. Soybean oil sales were tallied at 34,634 MT in the middle of the 20,000 to 60,000 MT estimates.

Jan 25 Soybeans  closed at $10.13 1/2, up 21 1/4 cents,

Nearby Cash  was $9.68 1/4, up 24 3/4 cents,

Mar 25 Soybeans  closed at $10.25 1/4, up 26 1/4 cents,

Jul 25 Soybeans  closed at $10.49 3/4, up 26 1/2 cents,

data.description:

Pork export sales totaled 31,038 MT in the week of January 2. Shipments totaled 25,474 MT in that week. The largest destination was Mexico with 8,300 MT, with 3,000 MT to Japan.

USDA’s FOB plant pork cutout value was 88 cents higher in the Friday PM report at $91.36 per cwt. The loin, picnic, and rib primals were all reported lower, with the rest showing gains led by the belly. Federally inspected hog slaughter for this week was 2.551 million head through Saturday. data.title: Cotton Fall on Friday as Stocks Build
data.description:
Cottonfutures posted losses of 70 to 149 points on Friday, as March was down 65 points on the week. The outside factors were mixed, as the US dollar index was up 481 points, with crude oil $2.69/barrel higher. Most of the focus was on the negative USDA data released this morning. 

This morning’s Cotton Ginnings report showed that 1.626 million RB of cotton had been ginned in the last couple weeks of December. That brought the season’s total to 13.051 million RB, the largest in 3 years and up 18% from last year.

USDA raised their cotton production total by 150,000 bales to 14.41 million, as yield was up 44 lbs/acre and harvested acres were trimmed by 360,000 to 8.27 million. With a 300,000 bale cut to exports, USDA raised their carryout projection by 400,000 bales to 4.8 million. 

The weekly Export Sales report was out this morning, with 137,382 RB of 2024/25 upland cotton sales reported, up 6.61% from last week. Turkey was the buyer of 62,000 RB, with 25,500 RB to Pakistan. Shipments totaled 191,672 RB, which was a marketing year high. Vietnam was the largest destination of 45,400 RB, with 43,800 RB to China.

The Seam reported 9,935 bales of online sales on January 9 at an average price of 64.75 cents/lb. ICE cotton stocks were unchanged on Thursday, at 20,113 bales of certified stocks. The Cotlook A Index was back down 25 points on 1/9 at 78.70 cents/lb. The USDA Adjusted World Price (AWP) was back down 37 points from the previous week on Thursday at 54.66 cents/lb.

Mar 25 Cotton  closed at 67.01, down 149 points,

data.title: Corn Rallies into the Weekend with Massive Yield Cut
data.description:
Corn futures closed out the Friday session with contracts up 14 to 15 cents across most front months, with September and beyond just 1 to 4 ¼ cents higher. March held onto 19 ¾ cent gains on the week Contracts are up 10 to 12 points at midday. The national average Cash Corn price from cmdtyView was up 11 3/4 cents at $4.38 1/2.

The annual Crop Production report showed the US corn yield from 2024 at 179.3 bpa, well below the average trade estimate and a 3.8 bpa drop from the November report. Harvested acreage saw a 196,000 acre increase, with production being slashed by 276 mbu at 14.867 bbu. The tighter production took the December 1 stocks to 12.074 bbu, which was down 97 mbu from last year and 73 mbu below estimates. The full year carryout projection from the WASDE showed an estimated 1.54 bbu of ending stocks, which was down 198 mbu from last month. Exports were trimmed by 25 mbu on the smaller supplies, with feed and residual down 50 mbu. The cash average price was raised by15 cents to $4.25.

On the world side USDA made no changes to the South American crops. Ending stocks for the world were trimmed down to 293.34 MMT, a drop of 3.1 MMT from December and nearly 1.5 MMT below estimates. 

The weekly Export Sales report showed just 444,950 MT of 2024/25 corn bookings in the holiday week ending on January 2. That was below the 0.7 to 1.4 MMT range of estimates, a MY low and down 42.7% from last week. Columbia was the buyer of 177,100 MT, with 91,100 MT sold to Japan and 77,900 MT to Mexico.

Mar 25 Corn  closed at $4.70 1/2, up 14 1/2 cents,

Nearby Cash  was $4.38 1/2, up 11 3/4 cents,

data.title: Cattle Extend Highs on Friday as Cash Leads the Front Months
data.description:
Live cattle futures were up another 42 cents to $1.37 on Friday, with Feb rallying $4.72 on the week. Cash trade was reported at $200-201in the South and live sales of $202-205 in the North and $320 in the beef. Feeder cattle futures posted gains across the board on Friday, with contracts up 75 cent to $1.10 and front month Jan up $3.125, on a stronger cash market. The CME Feeder Cattle Index was up another $3.55 from the day prior at $278.55 on January 9.

Beef export sales totaled 5,599 MT in the week of January 2 to kick off the new year. Shipments totaled 10,980 MT in that week. Japan was the primary destination of 3,300 MT, with 2,800 MT to South Korea.

USDA wholesale Boxed Beef prices were higher in the Friday PM report, with the Chc/Sel spread narrowing to $18.70. Choice boxes were up $2.06 at $332.84/cwt, with Select $5.79 higher @ $314.14. USDA estimated federally inspected cattle slaughter for this week at 589,000 head through Saturday. That is 83,000 above last week and up 54,098 head from the same week last year.

data.description:
** Shares of drug developer Sage Therapeutics rise about 33% to $7.38 in extended trading

** Biogen sends letter to SAGE CEO offering to buy all outstanding shares of the company it does not already own

** BIIB offers to buy all SAGE shares for $7.22 per share in cash, implying a total deal value of $441.7 mln, according to Reuters calculations

** BIIB, which is SAGE's largest shareholder, currently owns 10.2% or over 6.24 mln shares of SAGE common stock

** The share price offered implies a 30% premium to SAGE's closing price on Friday

** SAGE stock fell ~75% in 2024

data.title: Alleged Blender and Sinbad crypto mixer operators charged in money laundering case
data.description:
A Northern District of Georgia federal grand jury charged three Russian nationals for alleged crimes connected to operating two crypto mixers. 

The defendants, Roman Vitalyevich Ostapenko, Alexander Evgenievich Oleynik and Anton Vyachlavovich Tarasov, allegedly operated two mixers called Blender.io and Sinbad.io, according to a release from the U.S. Department of Justice on Friday. They have been charged with conspiracy to commit money laundering and operating an unlicensed money transmitting business.

Crypto mixers, services used to obfuscate cryptocurrency transactions, are a common target for law enforcement due to their alleged use by criminals. For instance, in 2022, the U.S. Treasury Department's Office of Foreign Assets Control sanctioned the Ethereum-based mixer Tornado Cash, asserting it helped launder hundreds of millions of dollars worth of crypto from bad actors, including the North Korean state-sponsored Lazarus Group.

The United States sanctioned Blender in May 2022, after finding that North Korean hackers used the mixer to launder $20.5 million from the roughly $600 million Axie Infinity hack. Blockchain forensics firm Elliptic noted in 2023 that Sinbad appeared to be a rebranded version of the mixer as it was nearly identical and likely operated by the same group, The Block previously reported. 

“By allegedly operating these mixers, the defendants made it easier for state-sponsored hacking groups and other cybercriminals to profit from offenses that jeopardized both public safety and national security." Principal Deputy Assistant Attorney General Brent S. Wible said in a Jan. 10 statement. He added that mixers serve as "safe havens" for laundering funds derived from criminal means like ransomware and fraud.

Blender maintained a "no logs policy" and deleted user transaction details. It operated from 2018 to 2022 with successor mixer Sinbad emerging a few months after Blender's closure. A law enforcement action shut Sinbad down on Nov. 27, 2023. 

The indictment charged Ostapenko with one count of conspiracy to commit money laundering and two counts related to operating an unlicensed money transmitting business. Oleynik and Tarasov recieved one count each for the same purported illicit actions.

If convicted, the defendants could receive up to 20 years in prison for the money laundering count and five years for each charge of operating an unlicensed money transmitting business. Ostapenko and Oleynik were arrested on Dec. 1, 2024. Tarasov remains at large.

data.title: The Stock Market's New Year Is Off to a Tough Start. What Lies Ahead, According to Our Roundtable Pros. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Lauren R. Rublin

One Roundtable, two crystal balls.

That's our assessment of the 2025 Barron's Roundtable, which featured a large group of bearish panelists and a smaller but equally committed cadre of bulls. It's hard to recall a year in which our panelists' views were so neatly divided — but then, it's hard to recall a January buffeted by so many economic crosscurrents.

First, to the bears, who expect 2025 to be far more challenging for the markets than 2004, which saw the S&P 500 index gain more than 20%. Why their gloom? Look to levitating bond yields, which reflect fears of resurgent inflation, and levitated equity valuations, which have left the stock market priced for perfection. The pessimists worry, as well, about the inexorable growth of government debt and the policy uncertainty that attends any change in Washington, D.C., especially the sea change promised by incoming President Donald Trump.

The optimists, on the other hand, expect stocks to keep rising, powered by a strong economy, double-digit earnings growth, government deregulation, an ensuing wave of mergers and acquisitions, and the widespread adoption of artificial intelligence by companies not only large but medium-size and small. The price action may be choppy, they say, but the ingredients for more gains are, or soon will be, in place.

The Roundtable met on Jan. 6 in New York, with the day divided, as usual, between our 11 panelists' discussion of the macroeconomic backdrop and their favorite investment ideas. Ironically, for all of the negativity at the morning session, most of these investment pros offered up more stock picks than usual, which suggests that the "market" may be overpriced but many stocks are cheap.

This week's Roundtable installment, the first of three, includes an edited version of the macro debate and the investment recommendations of Abby Joseph Cohen and Scott Black. Abby, a former Goldman Sachs strategist and current professor of business at the Columbia University Graduate School of Business, highlights two bargains in Japan and three in the U.S., while Scott, founder and president of Delphi Management, combs the small-cap market for a handful of unloved gems.

Barron's : The investment outlook seems riddled with contradictions. On the one hand, the economy is strong and corporate earnings are growing. On the other, the stock market is richly priced and bond yields are rising. David, help us make sense of this picture.

David Giroux: I don't mean to start this conversation on a down note, but we are probably as negative about the S&P 500 as we have been in a long time. Based on our company-by-company analysis of where stocks will trade by 2030, our five-year forward total-return estimate for the S&P 500 is less than 5% a year.

The market is trading for 22 times 2025 estimated earnings and 26 times 2025 free cash flow. If you break down the components of the index, you find that the market isn't just expensive, but it is broadly so. For example, traditional growth stocks, about 44% of the index, trade for 32 times earnings, versus a historical average of 28 times. The rest of the market, even ex-traditional growth stocks and removing the Teslas and Nvidias, trades for 18 times, versus 15 historically. Heavy cyclicals, including banks, industrials, and chemicals, are trading for 17 times earnings, a 10-year high, compared with 12 or 13 historically.

AI and AI derivatives were great stocks in 2024, and expectations and multiples remain high. The economic-expansion plays have had a big boom since Trump was elected president. Rockwell Automation is trading for 28 times earnings. Then there are what I call the Nifty 20 — low-double digit growers whose valuations are out of whack. Costco is trading at 49 times earnings, and Cintas, at 40 times. And then, there are the most speculative names, the Teslas and Palantirs.

As for where we see value, whether parts of healthcare, software, or parts of the utilities sector, the opportunities are the narrowest in probably 10 years. And, expectations of what Trump can and will do for earnings growth seem high. The 2017 Trump tax cuts drove 3% growth in gross domestic product and added nine points to S&P 500 earnings growth in 2018. This time around, it will be a monumental task simply to extend those tax cuts, let alone get incremental cuts. The deficit is north of 6% of GDP, versus 3.5% eight years ago. Our corporate taxes are about on par with the rest of the world, and Republicans have only a slim majority in Congress.

For all these reasons, we expect the S&P 500 to trade for around 18 times 2026 estimated earnings of $293. Our price target for the end of the year is about 5300. [The S&P 500 ended 2024 at 5,881.63.]

What would be the catalyst for a selloff?

Giroux: Expectations for earnings growth of 13%, or 14% ex-currency exchange, seem too high. There are deficit concerns and geopolitical flashpoints, and policy changes may disappoint.

Rajiv, we suspect you are less gloomy.

Rajiv Jain: I am more optimistic. In the U.S., broad-based valuations are on the high side. Who would have expected this, with interest rates moving up from around 1% to 4.5%? But capital expenditure has been strong. There is optimism about the prospects for deregulation under the second Trump administration. And if you look at the history of the first Trump administration, there was a lot of talk about tariffs, but many tariffs were diluted or negotiated away.

Outside the U.S., valuations are fairly attractive. Non-U.S. currencies have sold off. The fundamentals aren't so bad. There are some fiscal-deficit situations in places like France and Brazil, but corporate earnings have been better than expected. If you look only at the macroeconomic data in Europe, you would think corporate earnings are in the tank, but that is hardly the case.

Across the world, we look at banks. They are a good indicator of economic health, and they are in pretty good shape in countries such as Japan, Korea, Indonesia, and India. China is a little more complicated, but the large Chinese banks seem to be fine. I don't know how to interpret the collapse in bond yields in China; it is something to be watched. Growth in China isn't as bad as it seemed. We have gone from bearish on China to less bearish.

Non-U.S. markets disappointed last year. What would entice investors to move out of the U.S. and into other markets?

Jain: A slow rebalancing happens from time to time. It is hard to predict why, because markets can remain out of sync for a long time and adjust rather rapidly. We aren't bearish on the U.S., by the way. Corporate earnings have been strong here. While valuations are a little high, the companies that dominate the S&P 500 today are much less capital-intensive than the leading stocks of 20, 30, or 40 years ago. Nvidia at 30 times earnings isn't comparable to Exxon Mobil trading at 12 or 13 times earnings in 2007 and 2008.

Bill, give us your two cents.

William Priest: I didn't realize I would have a partner in pessimism, but I expect '25 will be a down year for the markets. Many valuation measures including price-to-sales ratios and price/earnings ratios would suggest we're at a peak. Inflation pressures are also an issue. They could pressure P/E ratios.

Francis Fukuyama's book, The End of History and the Last Man, was published in 1992, a few years after the fall of the Berlin Wall. In it, he argued that the world had reached a consensus in favor of liberal democracy. Countries embraced globalism and the law of comparative advantage as articulated by David Ricardo. Countries benefit from trading with one another by focusing on making the things they are best at making, while buying from other countries the things they aren't as good at making.

In the era that followed, we created the most efficient supply chain the world had ever known. But things didn't turn out as Fukuyama foresaw. In 2008, we had a global financial crisis, and today we are living in an era of deglobalization where the security of supply chains is deemed more important than the efficiency of supply chains. It is impossible for the U.S. to embrace this onshoring trend without having higher prices and fewer units. Tariffs, too, raise prices and restrict units.

In addition, throughout the West there is broad fiscal irresponsibility. This will create issues in terms of higher bond yields. Also, to run a big organization like the U.S. government, you need processes. The jury is out on what the next administration's capabilities are in this regard. All of this will put pressure on the market.

On the positive side, if you can substitute technology for labor and physical assets and hold your revenue constant, your profit margins and asset returns go up. That trend isn't going to change, and AI is a part of it.

Abby, where do you stand?

Abby Cohen: I expect that 2025 will be a difficult year for the economy, in the U.S. and globally. But my main concerns relate to financial markets that are priced for perfection, and the increased uncertainty regarding government policy. Market volatility will increase, and there may be some price declines. David thoroughly discussed equity-market valuations, but other valuations also seem out of whack.

In the fixed-income markets, credit spreads are extraordinarily tight. The last time they were this tight, [former Federal Reserve Chair] Alan Greenspan gave a famous speech about "irrational exuberance." Many people thought he was talking primarily about the stock market. But the speech discussed high-yield bonds and global fixed-income markets. There is now limited transparency in the private credit market, which is still receiving large inflows. I don't understand crypto valuations, so I won't even address them here.

What about banana valuations?

[Mario Gabelli has duct-taped a banana to the wall, a "replica" of the artist Maurizio Cattelan's duct-taped banana that recently sold for $6.2 million at Sotheby's.] Cohen: Exactly. I agree with Rajiv that some other markets around the world aren't priced for perfection, and there may be opportunities. There are also some securities in the U.S. that offer good relative or absolute value.

We are starting the year with a terrific canvas. The incoming administration has an economy growing close to 3%. Inflation has come down. Corporate profits and margins are at record levels. We have low unemployment, and real compensation rates are rising. I worry about applying policies that might harm this situation, or fail to enhance it.

Bill discussed tariffs, which can be inflationary and also depress economic growth among some of our trade partners. I would be particularly concerned about Europe, which already has sluggish growth, and Canada, and Mexico. I am also concerned about some of the regulatory changes we may see here, including the proposed reinstatement of Schedule F, which would allow the administration to reclassify tens of thousands of high-level civil servants as political appointees, meaning they could be easily fired and replaced by others without the subject-matter expertise. And there are some antitrust constraints we need to pay attention to. Unfettered approaches to mergers and acquisitions aren't sensible.

No one has mentioned immigration yet. In the decade prior to the Covid-19 pandemic, immigrants made up more than half of the increase in the U.S. workforce. Something needs to be done about illegal immigration, but we also need to encourage legal immigration, which has slowed. About 65% of the working Ph.Ds in the U.S. in science and medicine are immigrants.

A final point: Three pieces of legislation passed during the Biden administration were aimed at enhancing intermediate and long-term economic growth; growth is needed to improve the standard of living and reduce the nation's debt-to-GDP ratio. These are the bipartisan infrastructure bill, the Chips and Science Act, and the so-called Inflation Reduction Act. These three initiatives all need funding in the coming years.

Let's hear from John.

John W. Rogers Jr.: The stock market has been a tale of two cities. The S&P 500, dominated by large-cap growth stocks, is expensive, whereas small-cap value stocks are cheap. The S&P 500 is trading for 22 times the next 12 months' expected earnings, versus a historical average of close to 19. Small-cap value trades for 17 times next year's earnings, versus a roughly 10-year average of 17. The discrepancies remind me of the turn of the century, when everyone had to own the same internet stocks and small-cap value was left for dead.

There are very few quality analyst reports written on the small-cap value sector. No one is coming out of business school wanting to work as an active manager of small-cap value equities. In the 42 years we have been running money at Ariel, whenever people chased the same stocks for fear of missing out, irrespective of the underlying fundamentals, it was a sign that the market was near a top.

This is anecdotal, but lately more people are talking about how much money they have made in the market. I went to a Chicago Bears game recently and a proud dad was telling me that his son had made a million dollars in a matter of months in MicroStrategy options. My pizza delivery guy is talking about how much money he made in crypto. It sounds a lot like the dot-com era.

I expect the S&P 500 to fall substantially this year, by close to 20%. But small-cap value stocks could come roaring back and get the recognition they deserve. That is what happened when the internet bubble burst.

Priest: John, a large proportion of the companies in the Russell 2000 small-cap index don't have profits. The composition of the index has changed a lot over the years. Does that fact concern you?

Mario Gabelli: John isn't buying the index. He's buying specific stocks.

Priest: There is a struggle embedded in the small-cap value asset class because of this profitless portion of its constituency

Rogers: It creates opportunities for active management. We look for companies with positive cash flow, high profitability, and high returns on assets and equity. Maybe a lot of small-caps aren't earning money, but there are also wonderful small companies that have been neglected.

Scott Black: If you adjust the Russell 2000 price/earnings ratio for the 40% of components that don't make money, the P/E is 26.2 times forward 12-month earnings, not 17. Ironically, that is about the same P/E ratio as the Nasdaq, and higher than that of the S&P 500.

Todd Ahlsten: John, do you worry that some small-caps don't have the ability to adopt new technologies like their mid-cap and large-cap peers?

Rogers: We want to make sure that the companies we invest in have competitive advantages and what Warren [Buffett, CEO of Berkshire Hathaway] would call a real moat. Some small companies have the ability to have a technological edge.

Todd, are people really paying too much for the biggest growth stocks?

Ahlsten: We don't think so. We are still pretty bullish on the market. A double-digit return is our base case for the year ahead. Our year-end target range for the S&P 500 is 6500 to 6800, or 21 to 22 times our 2026 earnings estimate of $310.

Every year we look at three things: How does the S&P 500 look as an asset class? How does the economy look? How do liquidity and monetary policy look? Yesterday, I was jogging in Central Park at 8 a.m. It was a crisp 29 degrees. Fundamentally, investing in the S&P 500 is like running downhill. These are the best companies in the world. Their TAMs [total addressable markets] are growing, their moats are widening, and their relevancy is increasing. S&P 500 companies — whether hyperscalers, semiconductor or software makers, specialists in industrial automation, electrification, or autonomous driving — are unlocking productivity. That leads to expanded margins, multiples, and returns on capital.

While multiples are high, the U.S. is sucking a lot of capital out of global markets. Globally, growth is anemic. Demographics around the world are tough, with a few exceptions.

Deficits are running high, which is concerning, but if you look at the loan-to-value ratio of the U.S., it is less concerning. Productivity is the value. Also, we have energy independence and leadership in AI. U.S. corporations are generating enough sustainable cash flow to support the leverage we have put on the economy. At some point, our debt will be a problem, but as of now, the loan-to-value isn't going up on this house.

Last year, most of the gains accrued to the Magnificent Seven and a few other stocks. Do you expect the rally to broaden this year?

Ahlsten: It could broaden a bit. As of the third quarter of 2024, the Mag 7 were growing earnings by 30%. The rest of the S&P 500 was growing earnings by 4%. This year, Mag 7 earnings could grow at a mid- to high-teens rate. Earnings for the rest of the market could grow by low-double digits. It would be harder to have a big downdraft in stocks with earnings growing.

We are looking at 2% to 3% growth in the U.S. economy and low unemployment. Barring some sort of shock, the economy seems to be navigating higher rates relatively well. There is job creation.

Inflation is still elevated, which is concerning, but we are past peak inflation, and it is coming down. And, we are going to see monetary debasement around the world, whether in China or Europe or other countries that struggle to grow. That will add more liquidity.

What are the risks? Tariffs, a geopolitical shock, a problem with earnings. The economy could tank. There is a lot of exuberance. Typically, the risk comes out of left field. Our advice is to invest in great American companies with wide moats.

In AI, innovation has been the focus for the past two years. We have heard much less about the impact of the technology on business. When are the productivity benefits of AI going to start showing up?

Ahlsten: You're just starting to see them in software and the hyperscalers. Look at what Marc Benioff is doing at Salesforce, one of my Midyear Roundtable picks. The company has launched Agentforce, one of the largest suppliers of digital labor in the world. It allows companies to build and deploy AI agents across a variety of enterprise tasks and workflows. You will see the benefits over time in precision agriculture, industrial companies, and financial services — for example, in helping Mastercard to reduce fraud. There is a hype cycle, for sure, and the rollout could be bumpy. But look at the capital being deployed: $2 trillion in hyperscaler cloud capex could be deployed in the next five years.

Henry Ellenbogen: I recently finished my year-end tour of the companies in which we're invested. What most surprised and impressed me was the amount of AI-driven productivity this economy is going to see, not on a three-to-five year basis, but in one to three years. Looking at prior technology cycles, such as mobile and cloud, you didn't get huge productivity measured in GDP. From mobile, you got social media and applying new business models such as Uber Technologies and Airbnb. Cloud basically helped companies upgrade their infrastructure to gain better visibility, but didn't take out employees. What we are seeing in AI is jaw-dropping.

Three of my former picks, HubSpot, Toast, and Duolingo, really leveraged AI. Salesforce is the CRM [customer relationship management] standard for the largest global companies For midsize companies, the emerging CRM standard is HubSpot. Midsize companies need a centralized database of customer activity that underpins mission-critical workflows just as much as large companies, but this had been out of reach from a cost perspective. Now, with AI, HubSpot can provide much better value to its users while leaning out its own cost structure [making it leaner].

What do you mean? Ellenbogen: For example, 30% of HubSpot's customer service inquiries are handled with AI, which means employees can spend more time on more complex problems. In sales and marketing, 80% of inbound chat activity is handled by AI. As a result, HubSpot's revenue per employee is growing in the low teens, versus mid-single digits historically. Toast, the leading system of work for small and midsize restaurants, is a similar story. Using AI to lean out its cost structure, Toast added nearly 30,000 restaurants to its platform and grew gross profits 30% while only modestly increasing head count year over year.

Duolingo is a foreign-language learning tool that used humans to create content. Now it uses LLMs [large language models] and is therefore 50 times more productive in terms of content creation. Sweetgreen, the salad chain in which we are invested, is another example of productivity gains. By using machines to make salads with greater speed and more accuracy than humans, Sweetgreen can reduce store head count by 50%.

David recommended Aurora Innovation in the Midyear Roundtable. The company makes autonomous trucking technology. That allows truck owners to utilize their assets 24/7 versus humans who need to take breaks. This drives down the cost and time to ship goods. This year, we are going to see autonomous trucking in the U.S. Aurora is a great example of how AI helps companies lean out costs (human drivers) and drive more revenue (by taking share). On top of just efficiency gains, AI has created power-law companies and separated them from non-power-law companies.

What is a power-law company?

Ellenbogen: A power-law company is one whose advantages compound. In the case of autonomous vehicles, the more miles Aurora drives, the more proprietary data it collects, which then improves its driving algorithms. This attracts more trucking companies and shippers to become Aurora customers. In retailing, almost all the growth in the past 10 years went to Amazon.com, Walmart, and Costco because they were able to invest in technology, which gave them data and operational advantages. You will see this sort of restructuring across industry. The theme of the next 10 years will be productivity.

On the macro front, Abby made some reasonable points with regard to the risks of the Trump government, but if you talk to executives, regulation was stifling and applied inconsistently. Deregulation will be more impactful than tax cuts, and is coming at an important time. The U.S. is leading the world in next-level productivity enhancements that will drive these power-law businesses. Companies need to stop worrying about government getting in the way.

Not to be pedestrian, but what is your market forecast for this year?

Ellenbogen: The market's performance will be a referendum on inflation. If the 10-year Treasury yield stays around current levels, the market will grow in line with earnings. If yields go above 5%, we will likely have a down market.

The outlook could be determined by what happens with budget reconciliation and the extension of the tax cuts in Trump's first 100 days. Does Congress make an attempt to pay for them, or use sleight-of-hand accounting? If we pay for them in some real way, inflation will be reasonable this year and the markets will be strong. If we don't, for whatever reason, the bond market will be the police on the Republican sweep, and that will be tough for the stock market.

Sonal Desai: I am somewhere between Todd's view and the more pessimistic views at the table. But I am closer to Todd from a macro perspective.

As the fixed-income person here, I'll mention several themes, some reiterating points I made last year. First, once again, there may be a reality check on fiscal policy, with implications for inflation and bond yields. Since the financial crisis of 2008-09, the debt stock has been going up across the G-7 [Canada, France, Germany, Italy, Japan, the United Kingdom, and the U.S.]. Debt exceeds 100% of GDP in every country apart from Germany, which has a constitutional limit. In Europe, this is beginning to constrain what governments have promised populations in terms of the climate transition. The U.S. ran a budget deficit of close to $2 trillion last year in an economy booming by any measure. The last pre-Covid deficit was less than $1 trillion. Right now, there is no reasonable expectation of the deficit falling for multiple years.

Witmer: You don't think the Trump administration will make any progress cutting government spending?

Desai: Unless they tackle the heart of government spending — Social Security and healthcare — they can't make substantial progress. There just isn't enough discretionary spending to cut. I agree with Henry: If we can get the tax cuts funded, plus deregulation, it would be fantastic for the country.

I don't think tariffs will bring a major acceleration of inflation because tariffs impact relative prices. They aren't good policy, but they are just another tax on a subset of goods. And they are an inefficient tax because consumers can change their buying preferences, exchange rates fluctuate, and so forth.

Returning to fiscal policy, it is important to remember that the fiscal deficit has inflationary consequences. The buildup of inflation in 2022 wasn't all because of supply-chain disruptions. It was also the party the government had after Covid. The expansion of fiscal deficits fed into consumer demand, and the economy boomed. I don't see inflation coming down much. Core inflation is closer to 3% than 2%. It isn't a terribly high amount.

Will inflation rise much from here?

Desai: It will be hard to bring inflation down further. It is gratifying to see the market come around to assuming the Fed will cut rates only twice this year. It might be only once, or less, depending on what happens with fiscal policy in the next few months. The reason is the question of the neutral rate [the theoretical interest rate that neither stimulates nor restricts economic growth]. Last year, I said I thought the neutral rate was around 4%. I am sticking to that view.

I also said I expected the 10-year Treasury yield to end the year between 4.5% and 5%. I would say the same this year, with 5% probably being subject to upward pressure if we see a significant expansion on the fiscal front in the first 90 days of the Trump administration.

My next point concerns productivity. Productivity growth from generative AI was overhyped, but productivity growth from technology — Amazon and Walmart's robots, for instance — is real. In the past five quarters, productivity growth has averaged around 2.5%, up from 1.5% in the previous 10 years.

Will the payoff from AI in the next year or two come more from cost-cutting or from revenue generation?

Ellenbogen: It will come from both. We are invested in a private company, Podium, that sells "AI employees" to customers such as auto dealers and spas. They are designed to replicate the person who handles inbound email and phone calls and setting up appointments. It takes about 90 days to train an AI employee, but once you train it, it is more productive than a human. These are typically high-turnover positions, so the AI employee reduces resources spent training new employees. It also allows you to scale up your customer-facing resources at minimal incremental cost. Podium's technology is driving significant revenue growth and productivity for customers.

Doesn't this lead to a more concentrated market?

Ellenbogen: No. Initially, the businesses of the hyperscalers — companies including Meta Platforms, Alphabet's Google, and Amazon — are most immediately advantaged by gen AI. However, last year, the G2K [Global 2000] companies began investing in and testing it.

Now we are seeing smaller companies, the kind we're invested in, use AI to drive growth. First we invested in the infrastructure layer — the cloud-service providers and Nvidia — and now we expect the application layer to start showing significant growth.

In the examples I mentioned, we are seeing the baby steps of AI agents — a fancy word for technology that replaces specific human workflow — deliver better service and lower costs. This means that companies with differentiated data or products can move faster, gain share, and scale to further compound their lead. Expect the breadth of AI beneficiaries to broaden in the coming years.

Cohen: Sonal made several important points. I want to circle back to her point about productivity, which has increased in the U.S. mainly due to a substantial increase in capital spending, including for both hardware and software. At the same time, productivity has fallen in the euro area, the U.K., and Canada. In Europe, productivity gains fell from about 0.7% a year in 2015 through 2019 to 0.3% in 2020 through 2024, even as U.S. productivity rose from 1.3% to 1.8%. The U.S. is a magnet for foreign direct investment and portfolio inflows because of the investments made to improve our economy and our large end markets for products and services.

Mario, you've been too quiet. What is your take on the market and the world?

Gabelli: To set the stage, we have a $115 trillion global economy. The U.S. is No. 1, with a 26% share. The European Union's share has dropped to 17.6%, and China is 16.9%. So, how do we grow the world? In the U.S., the consumer accounts for roughly 70% of economic activity, with autos an important subset of consumer spending and housing being in the industrial group. We have talked a lot about government today. We have to rein in government spending and increase government revenues via growth in GDP, wages, and the corporate sector. My focus is the corporate sector.

What will revenue and profits look like? Labor is less than 10% of the cost of goods sold for most of the manufacturing companies we follow. That isn't a big challenge for overall gross margins. In my world, profits and profit margins will be reasonably good this year, excluding currency translation. I'm in the 4% Treasury bill to 5% 10-year government bonds camp on interest rates. And, I believe you're going to see confidence improving in the business world in the next 90 days.

With regard to corporate lovemaking [mergers and acquisitions], Lina Khan, the departing chair of the Federal Trade Commission, was a challenge. She ruled against so many deals. Under Trump's appointee, Andrew Ferguson, we will see many more transactions. I like that idea. We will also see more IPOs [initial public offerings], particularly if regulation is rolled back. The private-credit market could face challenges and potholes, as it is unregulated.

Now, I am going to give Trump advice on taxes.

That's just what he wants to hear.

Gabelli: He wants the Republicans to get re-elected in 2026 [in the midyear elections]. If a corporation spends $1 billion on capex, the company used to be able to write off as bonus depreciation 100% in year one. Now it can write off only 40%, and that will be dropping to 20% in 2026. If Trump wants to encourage capex, he should restore 100% bonus depreciation.

Second, you want to give an earned-income tax credit to individuals making less than X dollars, and I would increase the amount for lower-income W-2 earners. Third, raise the limit on deductions of state and local taxes, and eliminate carried interest. The 21% corporate tax rate is fine. It makes little sense to drop it to 15%. Last year, the U.S. collected $525 billion overall in corporate taxes on nearly $5 trillion of tax revenue.

On tariffs, I see a bargaining chip. The U.S. ran an $855 billion net trade deficit for the 12 months through Sept. 30, 2024, including $253 billion with China. If I want to negotiate with Mexico and Canada — on fentanyl shipments, immigration, and transshipments, tariff negotiations are a way to get Canada and Mexico to focus on these issues.

From an investment perspective, fasten your seat belt. The market will have a lot of wiggles, and you are going to have opportunities. The market could have a pretty strong drop sometime this year, but it will recover and end the year kind of flattish.

Meryl, what is your take?

Witmer: This is a robust discussion, but one thing no one mentioned is the possibility of a new Federal Reserve chair.

Jerome Powell is planning to serve as chair until his term ends in May 2026.

Witmer: He may, but sometimes things play out differently. A new person may have a new take on things. It might hurt the market in the near term but help the country and market longer term. Someone less dovish might reduce the money supply and not cut rates as soon, if at all. That, along with less government spending, if it comes to pass, should bring inflation down further. It would set the stage for lower interest rates in 2026, which would be helpful for the deficit and market. But more resolve, from Powell or a new chair, might make for a less robust market this year, at least in the short run. There are a lot of highly valued stocks and some with terrific valuations. I would expect some volatility, but to the downside early on.

Scott, the stage is now yours.

Black: Thank you. Economically, the U.S. is in good shape. I expect real GDP to grow between 2% and 2.5% this year. Core inflation will be about 2.4% as measured by the consumer price index, and 2.5% based on the personal consumption expenditures price index, so nominal GDP will be just under 5%.

Jay Powell was late to the party in recognizing inflation, but he has done a good job of controlling the money supply. The Fed's balance sheet was $750 billion at the time that Lehman Brothers collapsed in 2008. It later expanded to $9 trillion, and now it is around $6.92 trillion. On the monetary side, we are headed in the right direction.

Fiscally, however, we are on a perilous course. The U.S. debt is now $36.3 trillion, against GDP of $29.4. The debt-to-GDP ratio is at the highest level since the Truman era, and Truman inherited a mess because we had to fund wars in Europe and the Pacific.

According to the Congressional Budget Office, the deficit was $1.8 trillion last year. This year, it is estimated to be $1.89 trillion. Interest expense on the debt is now higher than the defense budget. Last year, it topped $1 trillion, against $822 billion for defense. Can we cut $1 trillion from the budget? Not really, because social benefits and defense spending are more than 80% of the budget.

If nothing can be done, why worry?

Black: We can't print money into perpetuity.

Things don't look terrific for the U.S. consumer. Credit-card debt is at an all-time high at $1.36 trillion. Student loan debt is $1.74 trillion. Credit-card defaults are up 50% year over year. The bottom one-third of U.S. households has zero savings, and more than two-thirds of Americans are living paycheck to paycheck. People don't have money to sustain the economy.

On the positive side, headline unemployment is 4.2%, the lowest since the Johnson administration. Wage growth is up about 3.9% year over year, ahead of inflation.

Turning to market valuations, analysts are always too ebullient at the start of the year. The current consensus estimate for S&P 500 earnings is $271.27, versus last year's $233.31, which implies 16.2% growth. The 16.2 % increase arises from S&P's own internal calculation of 2025 earnings, $271.27. My estimate is $261.00 , up 11.8 % year over year.

On that basis, the S&P would trade for about 22.8 times 2025 estimated earnings, versus a historic P/E of roughly 17. It is overpriced, as are the Nasdaq Composite and Russell 2000.

The 10 largest stocks in the S&P 500 have a 34.75% weighting. The Nasdaq's top 10 have a 51.08% weighting. These indexes are heavily concentrated. Most stocks aren't in the ionosphere based on price to this year's expected earnings; they are at 21 to 30 times earnings. I'm not saying that's cheap: My cutoff is a 14 P/E. But some deserve high multiples because of their expected growth. I don't expect multiple expansion this year, but earnings growth could propel the market. I am mildly constructive.

Will the Magnificent Seven become even more magnificent, or will investors spread the wealth? Or both?

Black: A lot of the money continues to flow into QQQ and SPY [ Invesco QQQ Trust, which tracks the 100 largest Nasdaq stocks, and the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust]. By definition, it will push those stocks up, along with everything else.

Ahlsten: We think the market can broaden. Several industries have been suppressed due to higher rates and pandemic aftershocks. Think about life sciences and housing, financials, industrials, even transports. As of the September quarter, the non--Magnificent Seven were growing earnings by 4% a year. This year, their earnings could grow by 12%, 13%, 14%. The Mag 7 could see decelerating earnings growth. The Mag 7 could still show nice returns, but our price target assumes a broadening of the market.

Black: I want to address John's comments. Every year, hope springs eternal for small and mid-cap stocks. They have systematically lagged since Jan. 1, 2007. From the start of 2007 through Dec. 31, 2024, the Russell 1000 Growth index has been the best performer, up 13.2% a year, compounded. The Russell 2000 Value has compounded at 6.3%. We are talking about a huge disparity.

What happened? Partly, after World War II, many small companies became successful and grew bigger. Numerous companies in the small-cap index today, outside of biotechs and AI-related concerns, are older, more mature companies that didn't have a successful strategic plan to grow. We can buy one-offs with good balance sheets and growing earnings, but as a homogeneous risk class, small-caps have changed dramatically.

Ahlsten: I want to throw John a bone, though. Small-cap value could benefit from a cyclical recovery and get a nice tailwind from a wave of M&A. The S&P 500 and small-caps could both go up.

Gabelli: Investment firms with sell-side departments don't want their analysts to cover small-cap stocks. They have to cover the big-caps. They can only cover 20 to 30 companies. The research focus on small-caps has to come from the buy side. You can find some real gems in the small-cap market. You just have to use small-caps as a surrogate for stock-picking.

Cohen: If I can summarize what I am hearing today, the financial markets thus far have been driven by momentum and have benefited from low volatility and low interest rates. The environment we are heading into may favor an "anti-momentum" market — namely, stocks with good absolute value or attractive relative value, and companies that can operate well with a somewhat higher interest-rate environment. About half the return of the S&P 500 in recent years has come from valuation expansion, which has been linked to low interest rates. In the future, it will likely come from earnings growth and pockets of good value. I suspect that our stock selection discussion will emphasize these fundamentals and diversification, not momentum.

Why wait? Abby, let's kick things off with your picks.

Cohen: In the U.S., stocks have moved up in large part because of valuation expansion. In some other countries, gains have been driven by earnings growth. This year, I want to consider markets outside the U.S. I'll start with two recommendations in Asia.

The first seems timely, given that congestion pricing has started in New York City, with the money raised going to the public-transit authority. My recommendation is Tokyo Metro, in Japan. The company runs the commuter rail lines in Tokyo and three surrounding prefectures, which represent the most populous portion of Japan. About 30% of the country's population lives in this area.

Japan's economic growth was disappointing last year but is expected to improve in 2025, which should translate to more business for Tokyo Metro. On top of that is the ongoing return-to-office trend, which is more robust in Tokyo than in many other global cities. People there tend to commute on mass transit much more than by driving. In fact, car ownership per household is down about 30% over the past two decades, while rail use is up. Increased tourism also helps, with people who visit Tokyo tending to take the metro.

Operating that network is the bulk of the business, but the company also owns some real estate in the Tokyo area and gets income from advertising on and near their rail lines. Tokyo Metro is expecting to see levels of about 90% to 93% of their prepandemic traffic over the next couple of years, with profit margins widening as usage increases.

How do the valuation and financials look?

Cohen: Tokyo Metro's price/earnings multiple for the current fiscal year, which ends in March, is 17.8. It is estimated to be roughly 15 for the following year. The stock sells for a modest premium to book value, about 1.3 times, based on numbers for this fiscal year and next.

This is a company with a high Ebitda [earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization] margin of about 39%. Earnings growth this fiscal year is forecast to be about 13%, and is likely to be in the high teens next year. It will start paying a dividend in March, for a 2% yield.

Are there meaningful risks?

Tokyo Metro shares became available when the local and national governments sold half their interests to the public last October. The risks that management warns of are related to natural disasters: earthquakes and tsunamis and so on, but also another infectious disease outbreak that would affect rail usage. Other potential risks have to do with rising costs for electricity and labor, and higher interest rates. But overall, the company has stable earnings over the long term.

My second stock is PetroChina. The Chinese market has been one of the worst performers in recent years, and now the expectation is that there could be a valuation recovery. But there is still a lot of nervousness about China, given its ongoing economic weakness and with the incoming Trump administration. Tariffs and other forms of trade policy could be detrimental. PetroChina's valuation is appealing and seems to take into consideration these concerns.

There are a few signs of improving economic conditions in China. For example, real estate prices in large cities seem to have bottomed out. There has been some recovery in industrial activity. And there are expectations of additional government policy stimulus coming. Also, with regard to trade, China has been aggressive in building alliances with countries other than the U.S.

The story here has much to do with attractive free cash flow. The stock's free-cash-flow yield is 10.4%, and its dividend yield is 7.8%. These yields are attractive compared with other energy producers around the world.

And, despite a 17% rise in price over the past year, the stock is still selling well below book value, around 0.7 times, and with a price-to-2025-earnings ratio of just 7.2 times.

Gabelli: Abby, what oil price do you assume for 2025?

Cohen: I'm using the Goldman Sachs numbers, which are generally flattish for the upcoming year. What matters for PetroChina is also natural gas, where prices also seem to be stable in the medium term. Jain: We quite like PetroChina, too. The company used to buy expensive gas and then sell it at lower prices to retailers and commercial customers. Now, the government is compressing the two prices so the losses on the gas side are basically going away. PetroChina is among the biggest beneficiaries of cheap Russian gas, which can't really be sold into Europe.

Cohen: I now have three suggestions in the U.S. Healthcare was the second-worst-performing sector in the S&P 500 in 2024, up only 3%. For 2025, I'm going to recommend the iShares U.S. Healthcare exchange-traded fund. It has an expense ratio of 0.39%.

A diversified approach is sensible because there are many investors who are underweight the whole healthcare category. This ETF has a 31% share in pharma, 21% in equipment, 18% in biotech, 12% in managed healthcare, and about 11% in life science tools and services.

The dividend yield on this ETF is 1.1%. For those concerned about the overall market, this defensive sector may be a safer way to participate.

The median stock in the sector is trading at about 16 times forward earnings, a 15% discount to the S&P 500.

Abby, does the recent public outpouring of anger around the healthcare system concern you from a policy perspective?

Cohen: Right now, there's policy uncertainty, as there always is when a new administration comes into office. That's already priced in.

The recent anger [expressed after the shooting of UnitedHealthcare executive Brian Thompson] is focused on the managed care sector, and yes, that is an issue. But that is about 12% of the total weighting in the ETF. The sector is dominated by pharma and healthcare equipment.

My next pick is exposed to the enormous and growing demand for electricity in this country. Some of this is related to electric vehicles, and a lot is from artificial intelligence and other uses.

We are experiencing a data center boom in the U.S., which isn't yet particularly noticeable in the national data, but some regions have seen significant increases in power demand. They include Northern Virginia, Atlanta, Texas, and New York state. Today, data centers account for about 3% of total power consumption. By the end of the decade, that will probably be 8% to 10% — potentially a tripling.

My recommendation is First Solar, which has been a volatile stock over the past year. The company has a significant opportunity from utility-scale projects, for users who generate large amounts of power — not the stuff that goes on a house rooftop. First Solar also partners directly with companies that own data centers, like Microsoft.

Importantly, it is primarily a domestic panel manufacturer, so that lessens the trade concerns. The incoming Trump administration could still negate some of the solar-power incentives under the Inflation Reduction Act. But First Solar stock is now trading at the same valuation it had before that legislation was introduced, so that may already be priced in. It benefits from other items, including the Solar Investment Tax Credit and the Section 45X Advanced Manufacturing Production Credit.

The stock is trading at about 10 times this year's forecasted earnings, with substantial growth expected. Revenue is expected to be up about 30% in the coming year, and earnings could rise 40%.

What is your last pick?

Cohen: My final selection is CrowdStrike Holdings. Cybersecurity is a growth industry and an obvious priority for IT [information technology] spend by most companies. Recent breaches have triggered even more interest in this category, including hacks of U.S. telecom systems and the U.S. Treasury.

CrowdStrike was in the news last summer when it was at the center of a large outage that snarled airlines and many other companies. It hit the share price, but CrowdStrike is still viewed as having a high-quality platform and has taken steps to rebuild goodwill with customers.

They have introduced flex procurement, which aims to tie their customers into several of their products and build brand loyalty. It seems to be working because companies are sticking with them: Flex customers have a 95% renewal rate, suggesting that while not all is forgiven, much has been.

Many people look at net new annual recurring revenue, which is essentially the growth in new customers. Those numbers have come down a bit. But earnings growth is substantial: In the fiscal year that ends in January, earnings are estimated to be up by approximately 20%. For the year ending in January 2026, they're forecast to rise about 22%.

The valuation is extremely high, as might be expected for the sector: around 80 times earnings.

Why do companies keep using CrowdStrike when attacks still get through?

Cohen: How many attacks have been thwarted? Targeted companies report that they can get cyberattacked millions of times a day. How bad would it be without systems from CrowdStrike and their peers? Nothing is going to be 100% effective.

Thank you, Abby. Let's move on to Scott.

Black: I have an eclectic list, oriented toward small-caps. AdvanSix is a chemical company that was spun out of Honeywell. It is based in Parsippany, N.J. The stock closed on Friday [Jan. 3] at $27.60. The market cap is $737 million. The company pays an annual dividend of 64 cents, for a yield of 2.3%.

As you know, I build my own financial models. Wall Street analysts expect AdvanSix to earn $4.11 this year. Our earnings estimate is $4.06. Revenue was about $1.56 billion last year; we see it growing 5% to 7%. Operating margins could increase from 9% to maybe 9.6%. That implies operating income of $147 million to $160 million. Assuming net interest expense of $11 million to $12 million, pretax earnings could be $135.8 million to $149.2 million. Taxed at 24%, AdvanSix could net $103.2 million to $113.4 million, or $3.87 a share on the low side, and $4.25 on the high side. Using the midpoint, the company is trading for 6.8 times earnings.

If you exclude stock-based compensation, which we like to do, the pro forma P/E multiple rises to 7.2. Return on equity will be 14% after taxes and return on capital, 11.1%. Book value is $28.67 a share, so it is selling for 0.96 times book.

This year capex will be high, at $150 million, but AdvanSix will generate $21 million of free cash flow. In 2023, they generated free cash flow of $185 million on $172 million in net income. Earnings are growing nicely, which we like. The past two quarters saw a turnaround in earnings and operating margins. The net debt to equity ratio is 0.26.

What does AdvanSix produce?

Black: Nylon 6, a synthetic material used in carpets, automotive and electrical components, sports apparel, and food packaging, accounts for 24% of sales. Caprolactam, the main feedstock in the production of nylon fiber, is about 19%. Ammonium sulfate is 27%; that's a fancy name for fertilizer. As production goes from liquid to pellet form, operating margins are moving up. The fourth business is chemical intermediates. These manufacturing byproducts are used in adhesives, paints, solvents, and such.

The U.S. accounts for 82% of revenue; Latin America and Canada about 11.9%; Europe and the Middle East, 4%; and Asia, 2%. Is this a great company, like the AI companies Henry discussed? No. But the business is improving, the balance sheet is strong, and it is selling for about a third of the multiple of the S&P 500.

We'll take another.

Black: Unity Bancorp is based in Clinton, N.J. It has 21 branches and about 220 full-time employees. Earnings have gone straight up since 2017. Unity finished 2024 with earning assets of about $2.59 billion. We estimate they will increase this year to $2.66 billion to $2.68 billion. The net interest margin, or the cost of assets minus the cost of liabilities, was about 4.16% in the third quarter, which is outstanding. Net interest should total between $110.4 million and $111.5 million this year.

Unity is liability-sensitive [liabilities reprice faster than assets in response to interest-rate changes]. For every one percentage point increase in rates, the bank loses about $2.5 million in net interest. Rates have backed up by about 56 basis points since the third quarter, so that would shave about $1.4 million from net interest, leaving the total in 2025 at between $109 million and $110.1 million. [A basis point is a hundredth of a percentage point.]

The provision for loan losses is only about $3.3 million. Non-interest expense is about $8 million The efficiency ratio [expenses divided by revenue] is about 44.2%, outstanding for a small bank.

We expect Unity to earn between $4.65 and $4.68 a share this year. The Street's estimate is $4.56. Return on equity in 2025 should be 15%. The return on assets is a sensational 1.77%, and the P/E ratio is 9.1, based on this year's expected earnings.

Priest: Does Unity pay a dividend?

Black: The stock is $42.46. There are 10 million shares outstanding, and the market cap is $425 million. The dividend is 52 cents a share, for a 1.2% yield.

Unity's reserve for loan losses is 1.7 times, so there is a margin of safety. The assets-to-equity ratio is 9.3 times, indicating that it isn't heavily leveraged.

Unity services northern and central New Jersey. Commercial real estate loans are 60.8% of the loan book. Residential mortgages are about 29%. Residential construction is 4.6%, and consumer is only 3.3%. The average interest rate on the portfolio is about 6.53%. Only 7% of the commercial real estate loans come due in the next five years, and the average outstanding loan is about $5 million.

Unity was founded in 1991. It has available liquidity of $569 million, so it won't go out of business if we have another crisis.

Are you expecting a wave of bank M&A, or smaller banks getting rolled up by larger banks?

Black: That's a good question, but we never buy on the basis of a takeover. We buy to own the existing business.

You may remember that the regional-bank stock index took off last year when the Fed indicated that interest rates would be coming down. Now that bond yields have backed up, putting pressure on net interest margins, the stocks have come down. But Unity is a good business. It was earning in excess of 15% on equity year in, year out, when most regional banks were earning 8% to 10%.

Next, Ultra Clean Holdings is based in Hayward, Calif. It is a developer and supplier of critical subsystems, components, parts, and high-purity cleaning services for the semiconductor and semiconductor capital-equipment industries. The company designs products for Lam Research and Applied Materials, including chemical delivery modules, frame assemblies, and process modules. In services, it performs high-purity cleansing of tools, process-tool recoating, and micro-contamination analysis.

The stock is $37.57, and the market cap is $1.69 billion. There is no dividend. Ultra Clean finished last year with roughly $2.09 billion of revenue. We expect revenue to increase around 13% this year, to about $2.362 billion. Above $2 billion, operating margins should approach 10%. That implies $236.2 million in operating income.

After interest expense of $48 million, interest income of about $5 million, and taxes paid at a 24% rate, we get between $129 million and $147 million in net income, or $2.86 to $3.26 per share. Our midpoint is $3.06, compared with the Street estimate of $2.77. The stock trades for 12.3 times our estimate. Back out stock-based compensation of 38 cents a share and the multiple is 14 times. Pro forma 2025 return on equity is 14.6%; return on total capital, 12.3%; and the price to book-value ratio, 1.97.

In terms of the business, memory was about 27% of revenue this past year. Foundry and logic was 56%, non-semiconductor revenue was 6%, and service revenue, 11%. In coming years, the company thinks memory will grow faster than foundry and logic. Ultra Clean has a small presence in China, but enough manufacturing capacity in the U.S. that it won't be hurt by tariffs. Operations are based primarily in the Western U.S., Maine, the U.K., the Czech Republic, Israel, Korea, Taiwan, and elsewhere in Asia. The addressable market is roughly $25 billion to $30 billion. The company expects to benefit from the increasing complexity of chips.

Ultra Clean likely won't ever command the P/E multiples of Lam or Applied Materials, which we happen to own. They trade for around 18 times earnings. But this is a good surrogate play with the wind at its back.

What else are you recommending?

Black: SS&C Technologies Holdings is based in Windsor, Conn. The founder, William Stone, still runs it. Delphi is a customer. SS&C is the largest hedge fund and private-equity administrator and mutual fund transfer agent. It provides end-to-end expertise across financial-service operations, with software and solutions. It operates via contracts and licensing agreements. Customers are asset managers, insurance companies, wealth managers, banks, and brokerage firms. The company also has a healthcare division that handles claims processing, analytics, member engagement, and the like.

The stock trades for $76.05. It has an $18.8 billion market cap and a 1.3% dividend yield. Top-line growth is only about 5% or 6%, but bottom line grows consistently by 10% or 11%. From 2018 to 2023, return on equity was consistently greater than 19%. The company has had only one down year in earnings growth since 2015. In 2022, it earned $4.65 a share, below 2021's $5.02.

The revenue base is about $5.84 billion. It could grow by 5.5% to 6% this year. We peg operating income at $2.33 billion on the low end and $2.36 billion on the high end, and net income at $1.42 billion to $1.44. After accounting for stock buybacks, divide that by 245.3 million shares and you get adjusted earnings per share of $5.77 to $5.86. My midpoint is $5.81, and the Street's estimate is $5.75.

We expect SS&C to generate about $1.63 billion in free cash flow this year. Return on equity will be about 18.2% for the year and return on total capital, 9.4%. The P/E is 13.1. After removing 84 cents a share in stock-based compensation, we get earnings of $4.97, and a P/E of about 15.3.

A few more details: SS&C has 27,000 employees, and 114 offices in 90 cities. The company spends about $500 million a year on internal software development. The revenue mix is 82% software and 18% licenses and maintenance. SS&C grows consistently and generates a lot of cash.

Thanks, Scott.

  • Additional editing by Nicholas Jasinski

data.title: SEC, Shift4 Payments Reach Disclosure-Violation Settlement
data.description:
By Connor Hart

The Securities and Exchange Commission reached a $750,000 settlement with Shift4 Payments, resolving allegations that the payments-technology company failed to make required disclosures in several of its filings.

The SEC said Friday that Shift4 failed to state in its annual filing and proxy statement for 2020 that the sibling of an executive officer, who was also a child of a director, received about $1.1 million in compensation as a non-executive employee of the company.

In the same filing, the agency alleged another sibling of an executive officer received more than $280,000 from the company for residual commissions while acting as an independent sales agent not employed by Shift4.

Shift 4 could not immediately be reached for comment.

The company failed to make similar disclosures in its annual filings and proxy statements through fiscal 2023, during which time immediate family members received more than $120,000 annually in compensation and payments from Shift4, the SEC alleged.

Under the terms of the settlement, Shift4 agreed to stop committing disclosure violations now and in the future.

Write to Connor Hart at connor.hart@wsj.com

data.title: Rexford Industrial Stock Ticks Up After Reporting No Wildfire Damage
data.description:
By Dean Seal

Shares of Rexford Industrial Realty edged higher after the Los Angeles-based company said wildfires raging in Southern California haven't damaged its properties.

The stock was up 1.5% at $37.73 in after hours trading Friday. Shares closed at $56.08 this time a year ago.

The real estate investment trust said after the markets closed that it doesn't anticipate unfavorable impacts to its operating results or financial condition as a result of the wildfires.

"We are prioritizing the care of our employees, their family members and the larger community and are fortunate all members of the Rexford family are safe," said Michael Frankel and Howard Schwimmer, the company's co-chief executives.

Write to Dean Seal at dean.seal@wsj.com
